title: Effective Noise Mitigation via Quantum Circuit Learning in Quantum Simulation of Integrable Spin Chains
authors: Wenlong Zhao, Yimeng Zhang, Yan Guo, Yufan Cui, Zhuohang Wang, Rui-Dong Zhu
abstract: We propose a noise-mitigation quantum simulation strategy for near-term quantum devices based on Quantum Circuit Learning (QCL), which is in particular effective for integrable quantum spin chains. The method trains a shallow variational circuit to approximate a deeper time-evolution circuit by learning the conserved charges and only a small amount of dynamical information in the system. Under realistic noise models, the learned circuit maintains both conserved quantities and dynamical observables significantly closer to their true values than the noisy simulation of the original circuit. This demonstrates QCL as an effective, physics-informed error mitigation strategy, producing shorter, more robust circuits without exponential sampling overhead.
summary: The authors propose using Quantum Circuit Learning to compress deep, noisy time-evolution circuits into shallower, more noise-resilient ones. By training the circuit to respect the conserved charges of integrable spin chains, the method effectively mitigates the impact of common hardware noise. This approach provides a way to perform more accurate quantum simulations without the high sampling overhead of traditional error mitigation techniques.
main_problem: Mitigating hardware noise (decoherence and gate infidelities) in the quantum simulation of integrable spin chains on NISQ devices.
main_result: Quantum Circuit Learning (QCL) can produce shallower, more robust circuits that effectively suppress noise-induced decay of conserved quantities and dynamical observables.
method: A machine learning framework that trains a shallow variational circuit to approximate a deeper, noisy time-evolution operator by leveraging conserved charges as a training signal.
model_or_system: Integrable quantum spin chains, specifically the XXX Heisenberg model, studied for small system sizes (L=2 and L=3).
key_observables: Conserved charges (total magnetization Z_tot, X_tot, Y_tot, and Hamiltonian H) and dynamical local observables (e.g., <Z1>, <X1>, <Y1>).
important_parameters: Circuit depths (evolution depth d and learning depth D), noise probabilities (p), and the initial state parameter (x).
main_assumptions: The strategy is optimized for integrable systems where conserved charges are available; the learning circuit depth D must be balanced to avoid accumulating too much noise.
figures_summary: Figures compare predictions of the learned circuit against true data, demonstrate the preservation of conserved charges under various noise models (bit-flip, depolarizing, amplitude, and phase damping), and show the scalability and reusability of the learned circuits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the noise mitigation problem, describes the QCL method and the use of conserved charges for training, presents numerical results for L=2 and L=3 systems under various noise channels, and discusses scalability and future directions.
why_interesting: This work presents a physics-informed error mitigation strategy that uses the intrinsic symmetries of many-body systems to improve the fidelity of quantum simulations, which is highly relevant for studying many-body dynamics on near-term hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Predictions from learned circuit (solid lines) compared with the original data for L = 2 and d = 15 (dashed lines). In particular, the data for ⟨X1⟩and ⟨Y1⟩were not taught to the circuit (n.b. in the quantum simulation, the first qubit is usually called qubit 0). The scaling factor a is optimized to aopt = 1.014819.

FIG. 2. Comparison of predictions for the training data, H, Ztot, and the dynamical observable ⟨Z1⟩at d = 4 using QCL with circuit depths D = 2 (solid lines) and D = 3 (dashed lines). Both setups achieve relatively good agreement with the training data, and give similar predictions.

FIG. 3. Comparison of predictions for the training data, H, Ztot, and the dynamical observable ⟨Z1⟩at d = 6 using QCL with circuit depths D = 2 and D = 3. The predictions of the D = 3 QCL circuit (dashed lines) are clearly better than those of D = 2 circuit, especially for ⟨Ztot⟩.

FIG. 4. Conserved Hamiltonian H at x = 0.3 under bit-flipping (p = 0.5%) and depolarizing (p = 1%) noise. The learned circuit closely follows the ideal curve, while the un- mitigated simulation quickly deviates as the time-evolution depth d increases.

FIG. 5. Hamiltonian H at x = 0.3 subjected to amplitude damping (p = 1%) and phase damping (p = 1%). The QCL-based noise mitigation effectively suppresses the degra- dation, keeping the predicted values close to the exact con- served quantity.
